programs.name: Adults with Physical & Developmental Disabilities Waiver (APDD)
programs.tagline: If your adult child has a developmental disability and needs daily care, this waiver can pay for home services instead of institutional placement.

localEntities.name: Local ADRC sites (e.g., SAIL/ADRC in Southeast: 907-523-4428), SDS IDD Unit, care coordinators via Alaska Care Coordination Network

programs.intro:
If your adult child (21+) has a developmental disability like intellectual disability or autism and needs nursing-facility-level care, Alaska's APDD waiver can pay for home and community services instead of institutional placement. The waiver covers care coordination, residential supported living, assisted living, group home options, and medical/supportive services — allowing your family member to stay in the community.

To qualify, your loved one must have both a diagnosed developmental disability and need daily help with activities like bathing, dressing, or medication management (called Nursing Facility Level of Care). They must also meet Alaska's Medicaid income and asset limits, which follow standard Medicaid rules rather than the higher limits used in Alaska's elderly waiver programs.

structuredEligibility.functionalRequirement: Must meet Nursing Facility Level of Care (a clinical assessment showing your loved one needs daily help with activities like bathing, dressing, or medication management)
otherRequirements: Diagnosed developmental disability (intellectual disability, autism, or similar condition)
Assessment by SDS Intellectual and Developmental Disabilities Unit

applicationGuide.summary: Contact a local care coordinator first, who will arrange home assessment and guide you through Medicaid application.

steps.title: Contact local care coordinator
steps.description: Call your local Aging and Disability Resource Center (ADRC) or SDS Intake office — they'll arrange an initial screening and home assessment.

steps.title: Complete functional assessment
steps.description: SDS Intellectual and Developmental Disabilities Unit will assess whether your loved one meets Nursing Facility Level of Care requirements.

steps.title: Apply for Medicaid
steps.description: Complete Medicaid financial eligibility application through Alaska's Division of Public Assistance (DPA) — your care coordinator can help coordinate this process.

steps.title: Wait for approval letter
steps.description: You'll receive notification of both functional and financial eligibility decisions before services can begin.

applicationGuide.waitlist: Likely exists due to waiver caps
applicationGuide.tip: Start with the care coordinator contact — they handle the complex coordination between different agencies and assessments.

categories.name: Medical
items: Medical records showing need for daily care assistance
Current medication lists and medical history

contentSections.text: This waiver requires both developmental disability AND nursing-level care needs. If your loved one is elderly without developmental disability, Alaska's ALI waiver may be more appropriate.
Available services vary significantly by region and local provider availability — your care coordinator will know what's actually available in your area.

faqs.question: What's the difference between APDD and Alaska's other waivers?
faqs.answer: APDD specifically requires a developmental disability diagnosis (like intellectual disability or autism) plus nursing-level care needs. Alaska's ALI waiver is for elderly adults without developmental disabilities. You can't qualify for both.

faqs.question: Can my adult child keep their SSI benefits while on this waiver?
faqs.answer: Yes, SSI and other disability benefits typically continue. The care coordinator will help ensure benefits coordinate properly without conflicts.

faqs.question: What if my loved one's income is just over Medicaid limits?
faqs.answer: APDD follows standard Alaska Medicaid rules, which are stricter than the elderly waiver (ALI) that allows income up to $2,901/month. Check with DPA about potential spend-down options.

faqs.question: How long does the assessment process take?
faqs.answer: The research doesn't specify timing, but you'll need both a functional assessment from SDS IDD Unit and Medicaid financial approval from DPA. Your care coordinator can give you realistic timelines for your area.

faqs.question: What if no services are available in our rural area?
faqs.answer: Service availability varies significantly by location. Some areas may have limited residential options or providers. The care coordinator will know what's actually available locally and may suggest alternatives.

faqs.question: Can family members be paid to provide care under this waiver?
faqs.answer: The research doesn't specify family caregiver payment rules for APDD. Ask your care coordinator about participant-directed options that might allow family member compensation.

programs.name: Program of All-Inclusive Care for the Elderly (PACE)
programs.tagline: All-in-one medical care, meals, and daily services for parents who need nursing home-level care but want to stay home.

localEntities.name: None listed for Alaska; search nationally via Medicare.gov or NPAonline.org for any new 2026 updates.

programs.intro:
If your parent needs extensive daily help with bathing, dressing, or medication management but wants to avoid a nursing home, PACE provides everything under one roof. Your parent gets all medical care, prescription drugs, meals, transportation, and personal care from one coordinated team. Because PACE covers all services through a single provider, you won't need to coordinate between different doctors, insurance plans, or service providers — one team manages everything.

PACE has no income or asset limits for eligibility. Your parent must be 55+ and certified by Alaska as needing nursing home-level care, but able to live safely at home with support. The major limitation: PACE programs don't exist in most of Alaska, so availability depends entirely on your parent's location.

For parents who qualify for Medicaid, PACE is completely free. Others pay a monthly premium that varies by program — often thousands per month, but still less than nursing home costs.

structuredEligibility.functionalRequirement: Must be certified by Alaska as needing nursing home-level care (extensive daily assistance with activities like bathing, dressing, toileting, walking, transferring, or eating)
otherRequirements: Live in the service area of a PACE organization
Able to live safely in the community with PACE services
Not enrolled in Medicare Advantage, Medicare prepayment plan, Medicare prescription drug plan, or hospice

applicationGuide.summary: Contact the nearest PACE provider directly, though none are confirmed to operate in Alaska currently.

steps.title: Find a PACE provider
steps.description: Use Medicare.gov's 'Find a PACE Plan' tool or call 1-800-MEDICARE (1-800-633-4227) to locate any programs serving your parent's area

steps.title: Contact the provider
steps.description: Call the PACE center directly to discuss availability and schedule an initial assessment

steps.title: Complete assessment
steps.description: Your parent will undergo evaluation to determine if they meet nursing home-level care requirements and can safely live in the community

steps.title: Gather documentation
steps.description: Provide proof of age, residency, medical records, and Medicare/Medicaid cards if applicable

steps.title: Enroll and transition care
steps.description: Once approved, PACE becomes your parent's sole Medicare and Medicaid provider — you'll need to disenroll from other plans

applicationGuide.processingTime: Typically weeks to months for assessment and certification
applicationGuide.waitlist: Possible due to enrollment caps — common in areas with limited PACE programs
applicationGuide.tip: Before starting the application, confirm a PACE program actually serves your parent's specific address — most of Alaska lacks coverage.

categories.name: Identity & Residency
items: Proof of address in PACE service area

contentSections.text: PACE programs are extremely limited in Alaska. Most areas of the state have no PACE provider. You must live within the specific service area of an operational PACE center — this is not a statewide program.

PACE becomes your parent's exclusive Medicare and Medicaid provider. They cannot keep other Medicare Advantage plans or prescription drug coverage. However, they can voluntarily leave PACE anytime and return to original Medicare.

faqs.question: Can my parent keep their current doctors if they join PACE?
faqs.answer: No. PACE provides all medical care through their own team of doctors and specialists. Your parent will get new primary care physicians, but the PACE team coordinates all care — often providing better continuity than juggling multiple separate providers.

faqs.question: What if my parent's income is too high for Medicaid but they can't afford the PACE premium?
faqs.answer: PACE premiums for non-Medicaid enrollees can cost thousands per month, varying by program. However, this is typically still less expensive than nursing home care. Contact the local PACE provider for their specific premium rates and any available financial assistance.

faqs.question: Does my parent have to go to the PACE center every day?
faqs.answer: Not every day, but regular attendance is expected — typically 20-40 hours per week. The adult day health component is central to PACE's model. Your parent must live close enough to attend regularly, which is why service areas are geographically limited.

faqs.question: What happens if my parent needs to move to a different city?
faqs.answer: They would need to find a PACE program in their new location, which may not exist. PACE enrollment is tied to specific service areas. If no PACE program serves their new address, they'll need to disenroll and return to regular Medicare.

faqs.question: Can my parent leave PACE if they don't like it?
faqs.answer: Yes. PACE enrollment is voluntary and your parent can disenroll at any time. They'll return to original Medicare and can enroll in other Medicare plans during appropriate enrollment periods.

faqs.question: Are there any PACE programs actually operating in Alaska right now?
faqs.answer: Our research found no confirmed PACE providers currently operating in Alaska. Use the Medicare.gov plan finder or call 1-800-MEDICARE to check for any new programs that may have started recently.

programs.name: Medicare Savings Programs (QMB, SLMB, QI)
programs.tagline: Helps pay Medicare premiums, deductibles, and copays if your parent's income is under $2,275/month for couples.

localEntities.name: Alaska Public Assistance Field Offices (locations via health.alaska.gov/dpa)

programs.intro:
If your parent struggles to pay Medicare premiums, deductibles, or copays, Medicare Savings Programs can help. These three programs (QMB, SLMB, QI) pay different portions of Medicare costs based on income — from covering just the Part B premium to paying all Medicare out-of-pocket costs.

Alaska has higher income limits than most states because of adjusted poverty levels. Your parent automatically qualifies for Extra Help with prescription drug costs, saving up to $5,000+ annually on medications. Because these programs coordinate with Medicare directly, your parent won't receive separate bills for covered costs.
programs.savingsSource: Part B premiums ($185/month) plus deductibles and copays for QMB; prescription drug savings through automatic Extra Help enrollment

countedAssets: Stocks (except some Alaska Native corporation stocks)

otherRequirements: Must be enrolled in Medicare Part A

applicationGuide.summary: Apply through Alaska Public Assistance Field Offices by phone, online, or in-person — processing time varies by office.

steps.title: Gather required documents
steps.description: Collect Medicare card, recent bank statements, income proof (Social Security award letters, pay stubs), and ID documents.

steps.title: Contact your local Public Assistance office
steps.description: Call 1-800-478-7778 to find your local office, or visit health.alaska.gov/dpa for office locations.

steps.title: Submit application
steps.description: Complete the Medicaid/MSP application by phone, online through Alaska DHSS system, or in-person at your local office.

steps.title: For QI program specifically
steps.description: Apply early in the federal fiscal year (October-December) as funding is limited and operates first-come, first-served.

applicationGuide.processingTime: Varies by office — contact your local Public Assistance office for current timeframes
applicationGuide.waitlist: QI program only — limited federal funding with priority given to previous recipients
applicationGuide.tip: If applying for QI, submit your application in October when new federal funding becomes available.

tiers.name: QMB (Qualified Medicare Beneficiary)
tiers.coverage: Pays Part A premiums (if any), Part B premiums, deductibles, coinsurance, and copayments

tiers.name: SLMB (Specified Low-Income Medicare Beneficiary)
tiers.incomeLimit: Individual: up to $2,015/month, Couple: up to $2,720/month

tiers.name: QI (Qualifying Individual)
tiers.incomeLimit: Individual: up to $2,140/month, Couple: higher accordingly
tiers.coverage: Pays Part B premiums only — first-come, first-served

contentSections.heading: Asset Limits: What Counts
excluded: Burial plots and burial expenses up to $1,500
Life insurance with cash value under $1,500
Certain Native American settlements (Cobell payments, food stamp income)

contentSections.text: QI program has limited federal funding and operates first-come, first-served. Apply early in the federal fiscal year (October-December) for best chance of enrollment.
Alaska has higher income limits than other states due to adjusted poverty levels, but exact current figures require state confirmation. Contact your local office for Alaska-specific thresholds.

faqs.question: Can my parent keep their house and car if they qualify?
faqs.answer: Yes. The primary home (any value) and one vehicle are completely exempt from asset limits. Only countable assets like bank accounts and investments matter for the $9,950 individual/$14,910 couple limits.

faqs.question: What happens if my parent's income is just over the limit?
faqs.answer: Alaska may have income disregards that effectively raise the limits — including a $20 general disregard and $65 plus half of wages if working. Contact your local Public Assistance office to see if your parent actually qualifies despite appearing over the limit.

faqs.question: Does this automatically help with prescription drug costs too?
faqs.answer: Yes. All Medicare Savings Program participants automatically qualify for Extra Help, paying no more than $12.65 per prescription in 2026. You don't need to apply separately — it happens automatically.

faqs.question: Which program should my parent apply for if they might qualify for multiple?
faqs.answer: Apply for the lowest income tier your parent qualifies for. QMB provides the most coverage (pays all Medicare costs), while SLMB and QI only pay Part B premiums. The state will determine the appropriate program level.

faqs.question: What if the QI program runs out of funding?
faqs.answer: Your parent would go on a waitlist. Previous QI recipients get priority when new funding becomes available. Consider applying for SLMB instead if eligible, as it has guaranteed funding.

faqs.question: Are Alaska Native corporation stocks counted against the asset limit?
faqs.answer: Some Alaska Native corporation stocks are exempt, but rules vary. Bring documentation of any Alaska Native corporation holdings to your application appointment for individual determination.

programs.name: SNAP (Supplemental Nutrition Assistance Program)
programs.tagline: If your parent is 60+ and on a fixed income, they may qualify for monthly grocery money on an EBT card.

localEntities.name: Local Division of Public Assistance (DPA) offices statewide.

programs.intro:
If your parent is struggling to afford groceries on Social Security or a pension, SNAP provides monthly money on an EBT card that works like a debit card at grocery stores. Because Alaska has more generous rules for seniors, your parent may qualify even if their income seems too high — households with someone 60+ can earn up to $3,258/month for a single person and still qualify.

The amount varies based on household size and income, but every $100 more in net income typically reduces benefits by about $30. Because seniors get special deductions for medical expenses over $35/month and shelter costs, your parent's actual qualifying income may be lower than their gross income suggests.

If your parent is 60+ and lives alone, they may qualify for Alaska's Elderly Simplified Application Project (ESAP), which means easier paperwork, longer certification periods (36 months instead of 24), and no regular check-in interviews.
programs.savingsSource: Amount varies by household income after deductions

applicationGuide.summary: Apply online at Alaska's Division of Public Assistance website or call 800-478-7778 — processing time varies by region.

steps.title: Gather documents
steps.description: Collect proof of income (Social Security statements, pension), ID, Social Security Number, and medical bills over $35/month if your parent has them.

steps.title: Submit application
steps.description: Apply online at health.alaska.gov/dpa, call 800-478-7778, or visit your local DPA office in person.

steps.title: Complete interview
steps.description: DPA will schedule a phone interview to review the application and verify information.

steps.title: Get decision
steps.description: If approved, your parent receives an EBT card in the mail and can start using benefits immediately.

applicationGuide.processingTime: Processing time not specified but varies by local DPA office
applicationGuide.tip: If your parent is 60+, ask about ESAP (Elderly Simplified Application Project) — it means easier paperwork and 36-month certification instead of 24.

contentSections.heading: Income Limits by Household Size (2026)
contentSections.footnote: 2026 limits for households with someone 60+ or disabled. Add $1,146 for each additional person.

contentSections.heading: Asset Limits: What Counts
included: Vehicle equity over $1,500 (if not used for work/medical/daily needs)
excluded: Primary home and the lot it sits on
One vehicle per household (or equity under $1,500)

categories.name: Identity
items: Social Security card or proof of SSN

contentSections.text: Medical expenses over $35/month can significantly lower your parent's countable income. Bring Medicare premiums, prescription costs, and other medical bills — these deductions could make the difference between qualifying or not.

faqs.question: Can my parent keep their house if they get SNAP benefits?
faqs.answer: Yes. The primary home and the lot it sits on don't count toward the $4,500 asset limit for seniors. Your parent can own their home outright and still qualify.

faqs.question: What if my parent's Social Security is just over the income limit?
faqs.answer: They may still qualify. Alaska allows a $358 standard deduction, plus 20% off any earned income, plus medical expenses over $35/month, plus shelter costs up to $1,189. These deductions often bring gross income below the qualifying threshold.

faqs.question: Can SNAP benefits be used for pet food or vitamins?
faqs.answer: No. SNAP only covers food for human consumption. You cannot use SNAP for alcohol, tobacco, vitamins, medicines, pet food, cleaning supplies, or hot prepared foods.

faqs.question: How long does SNAP certification last for seniors?
faqs.answer: If your parent qualifies for ESAP (Elderly Simplified Application Project), certification lasts 36 months with no regular check-in interviews. Standard SNAP certification is 24 months with periodic reviews.

faqs.question: What happens if my parent's income changes after approval?
faqs.answer: Report major changes like new work income or lottery winnings over $4,500. Small changes in Social Security or pension amounts typically don't need to be reported immediately, especially under ESAP rules.

faqs.question: Can my parent get SNAP if they live with me?
faqs.answer: It depends on whether you buy and prepare food together. If your parent buys and cooks their own food separately, they can apply as a one-person household. If you share meals and grocery shopping, the whole household's income counts.

programs.name: Alaska Energy Assistance (LIHEAP)
programs.tagline: Get a one-time payment sent directly to your parent's heating company to reduce their winter utility bills.

localEntities.name: State Division of Public Assistance (DPA) offices
Tribal agencies like THRHA, Sitka Tribe

programs.intro:
If your parent pays their own heating costs and has limited income, Alaska's Energy Assistance program can send a one-time payment directly to their heating company or utility provider. The payment amount varies based on household income, size, location, and heating type — but could significantly reduce winter utility bills.

Your parent doesn't need to be 60+ to qualify, but seniors get priority for processing. The key requirement: they must pay at least $200 per year in out-of-pocket heating costs. Because funding is limited each year, applications often close early when money runs out — typically between October and August.

If your parent is enrolled in an Alaska Native tribe, they may need to apply through their tribal agency instead of the state. Non-tribal members apply through Alaska's Division of Public Assistance offices.
programs.savingsSource: Varies by income, household size, and heating costs

summary: All household members count toward income
otherRequirements: Must pay at least $200 per year in heating costs
Heating costs cannot be fully included in rent
Household includes everyone at the address sharing utility bills

applicationGuide.summary: Apply online through Alaska's health portal or at local DPA offices — complete applications processed within 45 days.

steps.title: Determine application route
steps.description: If your parent is enrolled in an Alaska Native tribe, contact their tribal agency. All others apply through state Division of Public Assistance offices.

steps.title: Gather required documents
steps.description: Collect Social Security cards for all household members, photo ID, 30 days of pay stubs or benefit letters, and recent heating bills showing $200+ annual costs.

steps.title: Submit LIHEAP application
steps.description: Apply online at health.alaska.gov, email liheap@alaska.gov, or visit your local DPA office, WIC office, or senior center.

steps.title: Wait for decision
steps.description: You'll receive a Notice of Decision within 30 days. If approved, payment goes directly to the heating vendor or utility company.

applicationGuide.waitlist: Funding limited — applications may close early if funds exhausted
applicationGuide.tip: Apply early in the season (October-November) because funding runs out and applications close when money is gone.

contentSections.heading: Income Limits by Household Size
contentSections.footnote: 2026 limits at 150% Federal Poverty Level. Add $860/month for each additional household member.

categories.name: Identity
items: Social Security cards for all household members
Photo ID for head of household

categories.name: Financial
items: Recent award letters for SSI, SNAP, TANF, or VA benefits
Recent heating and utility bills showing $200+ annual costs
Rental agreement if utilities are included in rent

contentSections.text: Funding runs out every year. Applications typically close between October and August when money is exhausted. Apply as early as possible in the season.
Household income includes everyone at your parent's address who shares utility bills — even roommates who don't contribute to expenses. Make sure to report all household members accurately.

faqs.question: What if my parent's heating is included in their rent?
faqs.answer: They won't qualify if heating costs are fully included in rent. However, if they pay some heating costs separately (like electricity for baseboard heaters), they may still qualify as long as they pay at least $200 per year out-of-pocket.

faqs.question: My parent is Alaska Native — do they apply differently?
faqs.answer: Yes. If your parent is enrolled in an Alaska Native tribe, they must apply through their tribal agency (like THRHA or Sitka Tribe), not through the state. Non-enrolled tribal members apply through state DPA offices.

faqs.question: How much money will my parent actually receive?
faqs.answer: The amount varies based on income, household size, heating type, dwelling type, and geographic location. Alaska doesn't publish fixed dollar amounts — the payment is calculated individually and sent directly to the heating vendor.

faqs.question: Can my parent apply every year?
faqs.answer: Yes, but they must reapply each program year. However, funding is limited and applications close when money runs out, so timing matters more than eligibility.

faqs.question: What if my parent has zero income?
faqs.answer: They can still qualify, but must provide documentation verifying zero income. Incomplete applications get delayed, so it's important to verify all income sources — including confirming zero income.

faqs.question: Does the payment go to my parent or the utility company?
faqs.answer: The payment goes directly to the heating vendor or utility company, not to your parent. This ensures the money goes toward heating costs as intended.

programs.name: Weatherization Assistance Program
programs.tagline: Free home insulation, furnace repairs, and energy upgrades worth up to $4,000 to lower your parent's heating bills.

localEntities.name: Others via Association of Alaska Housing Authorities or AHFC

programs.intro:
If your parent's household income is below $39,100 (single person) or $52,860 (couple), they qualify for free home weatherization services that can dramatically lower heating bills. The program provides up to $4,000 in upgrades including insulation, furnace repairs or replacement, door and window fixes, programmable thermostats, and air sealing.

Because Alaska prioritizes seniors 55+, disabled residents, and households with children under 6, your parent likely moves to the front of the waitlist if they meet these criteria. The program covers both homeowners and renters across all of Alaska, administered through local housing authorities in each region.

Your parent's home must be their primary year-round residence and cannot have received weatherization services in the last 15 years. The upgrades are completely free — no loans or repayment required.

otherRequirements: Home must be primary year-round residence
Cannot have received weatherization in last 15 years
Cannot have received AHFC Home Energy Rebate after May 1, 2008

applicationGuide.summary: Contact your regional weatherization provider directly — processing time varies but seniors 55+ get priority placement.

steps.title: Find your regional provider
steps.description: Alaska is divided into service areas. In Anchorage, contact RurAL CAP. In Juneau/Southeast, contact Tlingit-Haida Regional Housing Authority. Other areas use Cook Inlet Housing Authority or Alaska CDC.

steps.title: Complete application
steps.description: Fill out your provider's specific weatherization application form. Each organization has their own version but asks for similar information.

steps.title: Submit income documentation
steps.description: Provide tax returns, W-2s, or other proof of income for all household members. Include Social Security numbers for all adults 18+.

steps.title: Wait for home assessment
steps.description: If approved, a technician visits to assess what weatherization work your parent's home needs, up to the $4,000 limit.

applicationGuide.waitlist: Priority given to elderly 55+, disabled, households with children under 6, and lowest income families
applicationGuide.tip: If your parent is 55+, mention this prominently in the application — they get priority over younger applicants.

contentSections.heading: Income Limits by Household Size (2025)
contentSections.footnote: Federal funding limits statewide. Limited state funding has slightly higher limits.

tiers.name: Priority Tier
tiers.description: First served regardless of application date
tiers.coverage: Elderly 55+, disabled residents, households with children under 6, Native American households, lowest income families

tiers.name: Standard Tier
tiers.description: Served after priority groups on first-come, first-served basis

categories.name: Income Verification
items: Tax returns for all household members

categories.name: Identity & Residence
items: Social Security numbers for all adults 18+
Completed application signed by all household adults

contentSections.text: Your parent cannot qualify if their home was weatherized in the last 15 years or received an AHFC Home Energy Rebate after May 1, 2008. These programs cannot be combined.
Renters can apply too — the program covers both homeowners and renters. The weatherization work stays with the home, but your parent benefits from lower heating costs while they live there.

faqs.question: What if Mom's income is just over the $39,100 limit?
faqs.answer: Some providers have limited state funding with slightly higher income limits than the federal guidelines. Contact your regional provider directly to ask about state-funded slots — they may still be able to help.

faqs.question: Can Dad apply if he rents his apartment?
faqs.answer: Yes. Renters are eligible for weatherization services. The improvements stay with the home when your parent moves, but they benefit from lower heating costs while living there.

faqs.question: Our house was weatherized 12 years ago — can we apply again?
faqs.answer: No. Homes that received weatherization in the last 15 years are ineligible. You'll need to wait until 15 years have passed from the original weatherization date.

faqs.question: How much will this actually save on Mom's heating bill?
faqs.answer: Savings vary by home size and condition, but weatherization typically reduces heating costs by 10-30%. In Alaska's climate, insulation and furnace efficiency improvements can mean substantial monthly savings, especially for older homes.

faqs.question: Does being 65 guarantee Dad gets selected?
faqs.answer: Not guaranteed, but seniors 55+ are priority groups served before other applicants. Your dad would be ahead of younger families in line, but still needs to meet the income requirements.

faqs.question: What exactly gets done to the house?
faqs.answer: Services include insulation, caulking and weatherstripping, furnace repairs or replacement, programmable thermostats, door and window repairs, ventilation improvements, and safety items like smoke detectors. The specific work depends on what your parent's home needs, up to $4,000 total value.
